content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
February 2L, L99O
Anthony L. Miller, chief Deputy
office of the secretary of State
1230 J Street
Sacramento, CA 9 5814
Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
our File No. I-90-04 9
Dear l,!r. lliller:
You have reguested inforrnal assistance with regard t9 any
reporting or other responsibi I ities arising under the Political
neiorn e6t ltte naglrrll as a result of the Secretary of state's
involvement in the Calj,fornia Voter Foundation (the rrFoundationrr ) .
OUESTION
1. Does the Secretary of State's involvement in the Founda-
tj.on, as incorporator, give rise to any reporting or other
responsibil ities under the Act?
2. If so, would such responsibilities be elininated by
renoving the Secretary of state from involveuent in the
administration of the Foundation?
CONCLUSION
1 and 2. The mere fact that the secretary of state is an
j.ncorporator of the Foundation does not cause the Foundation to
incur any reporting or other responsibilities under the Act.
1 co.r".rrr"nt code sectj,ons 81ooo-91015. AI1 statutory references
are to the covernnent code unless otherrrise indicated. 
Comrnissi.on
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regnrlations Section
18000, et seq. A11 references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California code of Regulations.
Infornal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
innunity provided by an opinion or fonoal written advice.
(covernment Code Section 83114 i 2 CaL. Code of Regs.
section 18329 (c) (3) . )
428 J Street, Suite 8OO o P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento cA 958o4-ogo7 a (916) 3 22-5660


Our Fi■e No. I-90-049
Page 2
FACTS
The California Voter Foundation is a nonprofit corporation
which has qualified for tax-exempt status under Sectj.on 5o1(c) (3)
of the Internal Revenue Code. It was organized last year to
receive private donations to be used to supplement voter reqistra-
tion outreach and educational efforts traditionally 
conducted by
the Office of the Secretary of State. These efforts include bus
signs, billboards, bill stuffers, public service announcements, a
tol,I-free registration foru request nunber, placards, contests,
private-sector regi.stration drives and so forth. 
The drives are
nonpartisan.
The secretary of State was listed as the incorporator of the
Foundation, but she has had no direct personal involvement in
either creating or organizing the Foundation. You and two forrner
staff nenbers of the Secretary of State's office were li.sted as
the initial 
officers of the Foundation. The only rooney received
by the Foundation has come from participants in a'voting seminar
held last year. No substantial noney has been spent to date.
ANALYSIS
The foundation would become subject to the Act if it
qualified as a conroittee (section 82013) or a controlled conmittee
(Section 82016.) Those sections provide as follous:
'rcounitteet means any person or conbination of
persons who directly or indirectly does any of the
following:
(a) Receives contributions totaling one
thousand dollars (S1,000) or nore in a calendar
year;
(b) Makes independent expenditures totaling
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more in a calendar
year r' or
(c) Makes contributions totaling ten thousand
dollars ($10,000) or Dore in a calendar year to or
at the behest of candidates or coumittees.
A person or combination of persons that
becoles a conmittee shalt retain its status as a
committee until such tirue as that status is
terninated pursuant to Section 942:^4.
. 
Section 82013.
rrcontrolled conmittee'r Deans a conmittee which
is controlled directly or indirectly by a candidate


Our Fi■e No. I-90-049
Page 3
or state Deasure proponent or shich acts jointly
rrj.th a candidate, controlled conmittee or state
measure proponent in connection with the naking of
expenditures. A candidate or state measure
proponent controls a conmittee if he, his agent or
any other connittee he controls has a significant
influence on the actions or decisions of the con-
Dittee .
section 8 2 016.
Under the facts which you have presented, the Foundation does
not qualify as a committee or as a controlled comrnittee. A
nonprofit corporation which engages in nonpartisan voter regi,stra-
tion is not receiving or rnaking contributions. 
(In re Welsh,
4 FPPC Ops. 78 (1978), copy enclosed.)
The nere involvenent of an elected officer in the incorpora-
tion of a nonprofit organization does not Dean that the organiza-
tion will be treated as a comnittee or controlled committee under
the Act. The organization would have to be recej,ving contribu-
tions to be used for politicaL purposes or making contributions to
candidates or measures.
You indicate that present and former DeBbers of the Secretary
of State,s staff are listed as initial 
officers of the Foundation.
If the officers of the Foundation act at the behest of the
secretary of state to control the expenditures of the Foundation,
they would be considered agents of the Secretary of State. If the
Foundation's funds are used to produce materials or advertisements
which prominently feature the secretary of State during an elec-
tion canpaign, it is possible that a contribution could occur.
(Baird Advice Letter, No. A-82-096, copy enclosed.) This could
result in the Foundatj,on being treated as a controlled conmittee.
However, under the facts presented, the Foundation will not incur
any reporting or other responsibilities under the Act.
The commission is considering adoption of a proposed regula-
tion on the subject of whether a nonprofit corporation organized
under Section 5o1(c) (3) Day become a controlled counittee.2 The
proposed regulation is currently scheduled for hearing at the
Iilarch 13 neeting of the Conmi-ssion.
2  A copy of proposed Regulation
rev■ew.
18217 is enclosed for your


Our FiLe No. I-9o-o49
Page 4
I trust this answers the questions that you have posed. If
you have any questions or require any further infornation, please
contact ne at (916) 322-5901.
Sincere Iy,
Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel
dzuL
By: l,[argaret W. Ellison
Counsel, Lega1 Division
KED/MwE/ aa
Enc losures
